name: opentelemetry
context: Vendor-neutral observability instrumentation, OTLP export, Collector pipelines, and semantic conventions.
info: OpenTelemetry is the vendor-neutral instrumentation and telemetry pipeline layer for modern systems. It gives agents a common way to create traces, metrics, logs, and baggage in application code, propagate request context across services, export telemetry through OTLP, and route or transform that telemetry with the OpenTelemetry Collector before it reaches Prometheus, Sentry, Jaeger, Grafana, Honeycomb, Datadog, or another backend. This ecosystem matters because autonomous agents need production evidence: they must instrument code, verify spans and metrics, debug missing telemetry, control sampling cost, and keep signal names consistent across services.
body:
# OpenTelemetry

OpenTelemetry is the vendor-neutral layer between application code and observability backends. Agents use it when they need a programmatic way to create telemetry, carry context across service boundaries, and ship traces, metrics, and logs without tying code to one vendor SDK.

## Tree

- [[opentelemetry/signals]] explains traces, metrics, logs, baggage, and context propagation.
- [[opentelemetry/nodejs]] instruments Node.js applications with the OpenTelemetry JavaScript SDK.
- [[opentelemetry/collector]] receives, processes, and exports telemetry through configurable pipelines.
- [[opentelemetry/otlp]] covers OTLP endpoints, transports, paths, headers, and environment variables.
- [[opentelemetry/semantic-conventions]] defines stable attribute names like `service.name`, `http.request.method`, and `deployment.environment.name`.
- [[opentelemetry/sampling]] controls telemetry volume with head, parent-based, ratio, and tail sampling.
- [[opentelemetry/troubleshooting]] gives a field checklist for missing spans, broken propagation, and collector failures.

## Fast Path

For a Node.js service exporting to a local Collector:

```bash
npm install @opentelemetry/api @opentelemetry/sdk-node @opentelemetry/auto-instrumentations-node @opentelemetry/exporter-trace-otlp-http @opentelemetry/exporter-metrics-otlp-http @opentelemetry/sdk-metrics @opentelemetry/resources @opentelemetry/semantic-conventions
node nodes/opentelemetry/nodejs/scripts/create-node-bootstrap.mjs --service-name api --out instrumentation.mjs
OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:4318 node --import ./instrumentation.mjs ./server.js
```

For a local Collector that accepts OTLP and prints telemetry while you verify the app:

```bash
python3 nodes/opentelemetry/collector/scripts/collector-config.py render --out otelcol.yaml --debug
otelcol validate --config=otelcol.yaml
otelcol --config=otelcol.yaml
```

## Decision Model

Use the SDK in application code when the process can emit telemetry directly. Use the Collector when you need a stable endpoint, retries, batching, fan-out to multiple vendors, redaction, resource enrichment, Kubernetes metadata, or tail sampling. Use semantic conventions before inventing names; stable names make queries portable across backends and make [[prometheus/query]], [[sentry/capture]], and trace search agree on the same facts.

## Important Environment Variables

```bash
export OTEL_SERVICE_NAME=checkout-api
export OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES=deployment.environment.name=production,service.version=1.7.3
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=https://otel-gateway.example.com:4318
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL=http/protobuf
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS=authorization=Bearer%20$OTEL_TOKEN
export OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER=parentbased_traceidratio
export OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER_ARG=0.10
```

Prefer `OTEL_SERVICE_NAME` over embedding the service name in code when deploying the same artifact to many environments. Prefer `OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES` for environment, version, region, cluster, and team metadata.

sections.title: Agent jobs
sections.body: The main agent jobs are: add SDK initialization, add manual spans around important operations, configure OTLP endpoints, generate Collector configs, validate collector pipelines, keep attributes aligned with [[opentelemetry/semantic-conventions]], tune [[opentelemetry/sampling]], and debug missing telemetry with [[opentelemetry/troubleshooting]].

sections.title: Architecture
sections.body: Application code uses an OpenTelemetry API and SDK. Instrumentation libraries create spans, metric measurements, and log records. Propagators inject and extract context, usually W3C `traceparent` and `baggage` headers. Exporters send telemetry through [[opentelemetry/otlp]] directly to a backend or to [[opentelemetry/collector]], where receivers, processors, exporters, extensions, and service pipelines control delivery.

sections.title: When to use the Collector
sections.body: Use [[opentelemetry/collector]] when multiple apps need one telemetry endpoint, when outbound network policy should be centralized, when signals need batching or retry queues, when processors must enrich or redact data, when one stream must fan out to multiple backends, or when tail sampling must decide after seeing the whole trace.

sections.title: Safety rules
sections.body: Never put secrets, API keys, raw tokens, or personal data into span attributes, baggage, metric labels, or resource attributes. Keep metric and attribute cardinality bounded. Use route templates like `/users/:id` instead of concrete URLs with IDs. Use sampling for high-volume traces, but keep errors and important flows observable through explicit policies.

links.context: Understand traces, metrics, logs, baggage, and context propagation
Instrument Node.js services with the JavaScript SDK
Route, batch, transform, validate, and export telemetry
Configure standard OTLP export from SDKs and collectors
Use stable resource, span, metric, and log attributes
Control telemetry volume and tracing cost
Debug missing telemetry and broken pipelines
Prometheus commonly receives metrics produced by OpenTelemetry pipelines
Sentry can consume errors and performance telemetry alongside OpenTelemetry
Node.js services are a common OpenTelemetry instrumentation target
Collectors are commonly deployed as DaemonSets, sidecars, or gateways

related.note: Prometheus is a backend and query layer for metrics that OpenTelemetry can produce or collect
Sentry handles error and performance workflows that can coexist with OpenTelemetry traces
Collector deployment, service discovery, and resource enrichment often happen in Kubernetes
The Node.js skill is needed for JavaScript service instrumentation
